---
title: "The Impact of the 2022 NCUA Risk-Based Capital Rule on Complex Credit Unions"
subtitle: "Research Briefing — Internal Use"
date: "April 2026"
output:
  html_document:
    theme: flatly
    highlight: tango
    toc: true
    toc_float:
      collapsed: false
      smooth_scroll: true
    toc_depth: 2
    number_sections: false
    df_print: kable
    code_folding: hide
    self_contained: true
    css: exec_briefing.css
    pandoc_args:
      - "--wrap=none"
  # pdf_document: uncomment below to enable PDF output (requires tinytex)
params:
  fig_dir: "output/paper/figures"
  table_dir: "output/paper/tables"
---


```{r setup, include=FALSE}
knitr::opts_chunk$set(
  echo        = FALSE,
  warning     = FALSE,
  message     = FALSE,
  out.width   = "100%",
  fig.align   = "center",
  dpi         = 150
)

# ── Core packages ──────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
library(tidyverse)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(scales)

# ── Figure directory (set via params or default) ───────────────────────────────
# Run 4_Paper_Tables.R before knitting to populate output/paper/figures/
FIG_DIR   <- params$fig_dir
TABLE_DIR <- params$table_dir

# ── Helper: safe figure inclusion ─────────────────────────────────────────────
# Returns knitr::include_graphics() if file exists; placeholder message if not.
fig_include <- function(filename) {
  path <- file.path(FIG_DIR, filename)
  if (isTRUE(file.exists(path))) {
    tryCatch(
      include_graphics(path),
      error = function(e) {
        knitr::asis_output(paste0(
          "<div style='border:2px dashed #DDE3EE;border-radius:8px;",
          "padding:32px;text-align:center;color:#9BA8C0;background:#F8F9FC'>",
          "<p><strong>", filename, "</strong></p>",
          "<p style='font-size:12px'>Could not load: ", conditionMessage(e), "</p>",
          "</div>"
        ))
      }
    )
  } else {
    knitr::asis_output(paste0(
      "\n\n<div style='border:2px dashed #DDE3EE;border-radius:8px;",
      "padding:32px;text-align:center;color:#9BA8C0;background:#F8F9FC'>",
      "<p>&#128202; Figure not found: <strong>", filename, "</strong></p>",
      "<p style='font-size:12px'>Run <code>4_Paper_Tables.R</code> ",
      "to generate figures.</p>",
      "</div>\n\n"
    ))
  }
}

# ── Helper: load a CSV table safely ───────────────────────────────────────────
load_table <- function(filename) {
  path <- file.path(TABLE_DIR, filename)
  if (file.exists(path)) read_csv(path, show_col_types = FALSE)
  else tibble(Note = paste("Table not found:", filename, "— run 4_Paper_Tables.R"))
}

# ── Color palette (consistent with paper) ────────────────────────────────────
COL_NAVY  <- "#1B3A6B"
COL_RED   <- "#C94040"
COL_AMBER <- "#E8A838"
COL_BLUE  <- "#4A7CB5"
```

<style>
body { font-family: "Source Sans 3", "Helvetica Neue", Arial, sans-serif; }
h1.title { font-family: Georgia, serif; font-size: 28px; color: #1B3A6B; }
h2 { font-family: Georgia, serif; color: #1B3A6B; border-bottom: 2px solid #DDE3EE;
     padding-bottom: 6px; margin-top: 40px; }
h3 { color: #2A3A5E; font-size: 14px; text-transform: uppercase;
     letter-spacing: 1.5px; margin-top: 24px; font-weight: 700; }
.score-row { display: flex; gap: 16px; flex-wrap: wrap; margin: 20px 0; }
.score-card { flex: 1; min-width: 160px; border-radius: 8px; padding: 18px 16px;
              border: 1px solid #DDE3EE; background: #F4F6FA; border-top: 4px solid; }
.score-val  { font-family: Georgia, serif; font-size: 26px; font-weight: 700;
              color: #1B3A6B; line-height: 1; margin-bottom: 4px; }
.score-lbl  { font-size: 11.5px; color: #6B7A99; line-height: 1.4; }
.legend-row { display: flex; gap: 10px; flex-wrap: wrap; padding: 14px 20px;
              background: white; border: 1px solid #DDE3EE; border-radius: 6px;
              margin: 16px 0; align-items: center; }
.leg-lbl { font-size: 10px; text-transform: uppercase; letter-spacing: 2px;
           color: #6B7A99; font-weight: 700; margin-right: 4px; }
</style>


# Summary of Findings {#exec-summary}

<div style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white;padding:36px 40px;border-radius:10px;margin-bottom:28px;position:relative;overflow:hidden"><p style="font-size:10px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:3px;color:#E8A838;font-weight:700;margin:0 0 14px">Internal Briefing &middot; NCUA Office of the Chief Economist &middot; April 2026</p><p style="font-size:15px;color:rgba(255,255,255,0.65);max-width:700px;margin:0;line-height:1.65">The 2022 NCUA Risk-Based Capital rule imposed significantly higher capital requirements on the 696 credit unions with average assets above $500 million. Using a rigorous difference-in-differences design that controls for macroeconomic conditions, pre-existing trends, and thousands of smaller credit unions unaffected by the rule, this analysis documents the rule&#39;s effects across five outcome domains: capital adequacy, loan pricing, credit availability, portfolio composition, and profitability.</p></div>
<div class="score-row"><div class="score-card" style="border-top-color:#C94040"><div class="score-val">+73bp</div><div class="score-lbl">Mortgage spread increase over Treasury benchmarks</div></div><div class="score-card" style="border-top-color:#C94040"><div class="score-val">$325.9B</div><div class="score-lbl">Foregone 4-year member welfare under repeal baseline</div></div><div class="score-card" style="border-top-color:#1B3A6B"><div class="score-val">&#x2212;26bp</div><div class="score-lbl">Return on assets decline (31% of pre-rule mean)</div></div><div class="score-card" style="border-top-color:#E8A838"><div class="score-val">&#x2212;0.3pp</div><div class="score-lbl">Rule&#39;s actual protection at 2008-crisis stress level</div></div></div>
<div class="legend-row"><span class="leg-lbl">Inference key:</span><span style="background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404033;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:3px;font-size:10.5px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">&#x25BC; Concern</span><span style="background:#FFFBF0;color:#B8821A;border:1px solid #E8A83833;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:3px;font-size:10.5px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">&#x25C6; Mixed</span><span style="background:#F0FFF5;color:#2E7D4F;border:1px solid #2E7D4F33;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:3px;font-size:10.5px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">&#x25B2; Positive</span><span style="background:#F0F5FF;color:#2A5A9B;border:1px solid #4A7CB533;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:3px;font-size:10.5px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">&#x25CF; Baseline</span><span style="background:#FFF8F0;color:#8B4513;border:1px solid #8B451333;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:3px;font-size:10.5px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">&#x2717; No effect</span><span style="font-size:11px;color:#6B7A99;font-style:italic;margin-left:4px">&#8212; appears in every Key Numbers and Model Results table</span></div>

**Central finding:** The rule imposed large, persistent costs on member-borrowers through higher loan rates, reduced credit availability, and deteriorating profitability — while providing only marginal protective benefit over and above what existed before the rule. An empirically calibrated threshold of **8.5%**, rather than 10%, would deliver equivalent protection against institutional failure at approximately one-third the compliance burden. Finding 12 explains *why* the rule's effects were larger and more persistent than the 2008 financial crisis itself — through five structural mechanisms that distinguish permanent regulatory cost changes from temporary economic shocks.

---

# Part I: Research Questions & Answers {#rqs}


---

## Research Question 1 {#rq1}

<div style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white;padding:20px 24px;border-radius:8px;margin-bottom:16px">
<p style="font-size:10px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2.5px;opacity:0.7;margin:0 0 6px;font-family:monospace">Research Question 1</p>
<p style="font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:18px;font-weight:700;margin:0;line-height:1.35">What was the average capital buffer for complex credit unions before the RBC rule?</p>
</div>

<div style="display:flex;gap:14px;margin-bottom:16px;flex-wrap:wrap">
<div style="flex:1;min-width:260px;border-left:5px solid #1B3A6B;background:#F4F6FA;padding:14px 18px;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0">
<p style="font-size:9.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:1.5px;color:#6B7A99;font-weight:700;margin:0 0 4px">Answer</p>
<p style="font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:15px;font-weight:700;color:#1A2035;margin:0">72 basis points — critically thin</p>
</div>
<div style="background:#F0F5FF;border:1px solid #4A7CB544;padding:14px 18px;border-radius:6px;min-width:200px">
<p style="font-size:11px;font-weight:700;color:#2A5A9B;font-family:monospace;margin:0 0 4px">●&nbsp;Baseline</p>
<p style="font-size:11px;color:#2A5A9B;margin:0;line-height:1.4">Baseline context — explains why the rule had such bite</p>
</div>
</div>

Before the rule took effect, the average complex credit union — that is, any credit union with more than $500 million in assets, of which there were 696 — held a net worth ratio of 10.72%. The RBC rule required these institutions to maintain at least 10% to be classified as "well-capitalized." That left the average institution with a buffer of just 72 basis points (less than three-quarters of one percent) above the new threshold. Nearly half (47.6%) of complex credit unions were already below 10%, meaning they entered the rule period already falling short of the new standard and facing immediate pressure to build capital. This thin buffer is what gave the rule its bite — and explains both why some institutions struggled to comply and why compliance was so costly.

*→ See [Finding 1: Capital Built — But Many Institutions Still Can't Cross the Bar](#finding1)*


---

## Research Question 2 {#rq2}

<div style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white;padding:20px 24px;border-radius:8px;margin-bottom:16px">
<p style="font-size:10px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2.5px;opacity:0.7;margin:0 0 6px;font-family:monospace">Research Question 2</p>
<p style="font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:18px;font-weight:700;margin:0;line-height:1.35">Are complex credit unions now holding larger capital buffers?</p>
</div>

<div style="display:flex;gap:14px;margin-bottom:16px;flex-wrap:wrap">
<div style="flex:1;min-width:260px;border-left:5px solid #1B3A6B;background:#F4F6FA;padding:14px 18px;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0">
<p style="font-size:9.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:1.5px;color:#6B7A99;font-weight:700;margin:0 0 4px">Answer</p>
<p style="font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:15px;font-weight:700;color:#1A2035;margin:0">Yes on average — but the average hides a critical distributional problem</p>
</div>
<div style="background:#FFFBF0;border:1px solid #E8A83844;padding:14px 18px;border-radius:6px;min-width:200px">
<p style="font-size:11px;font-weight:700;color:#B8821A;font-family:monospace;margin:0 0 4px">◆&nbsp;Mixed</p>
<p style="font-size:11px;color:#B8821A;margin:0;line-height:1.4">Average improved, pass rate declined — the rule worked for strong CUs, hurt thin-buffer CUs</p>
</div>
</div>

The average net worth ratio rose by 46 basis points relative to trend. However, the probability of being classified as 'well-capitalized' actually fell by 3.7 percentage points. Institutions that were already well above 10% built larger buffers, while institutions near the threshold — the ones the rule was most designed to protect — actually saw capital ratios decline. Among near-threshold credit unions ($400M–$600M in assets), capital ratios fell by 44 basis points.

*→ See [Finding 1: Capital Built — But Many Institutions Still Can't Cross the Bar](#finding1)*


---

## Research Question 3 {#rq3}

<div style="background:#C94040;color:white;padding:20px 24px;border-radius:8px;margin-bottom:16px">
<p style="font-size:10px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2.5px;opacity:0.7;margin:0 0 6px;font-family:monospace">Research Question 3</p>
<p style="font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:18px;font-weight:700;margin:0;line-height:1.35">Did loan rate spreads change significantly after the RBC rule?</p>
</div>

<div style="display:flex;gap:14px;margin-bottom:16px;flex-wrap:wrap">
<div style="flex:1;min-width:260px;border-left:5px solid #C94040;background:#F4F6FA;padding:14px 18px;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0">
<p style="font-size:9.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:1.5px;color:#6B7A99;font-weight:700;margin:0 0 4px">Answer</p>
<p style="font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:15px;font-weight:700;color:#1A2035;margin:0">Yes — across every loan type, by large and economically meaningful amounts</p>
</div>
<div style="background:#FFF0F0;border:1px solid #C9404044;padding:14px 18px;border-radius:6px;min-width:200px">
<p style="font-size:11px;font-weight:700;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;margin:0 0 4px">▼&nbsp;Concern</p>
<p style="font-size:11px;color:#C94040;margin:0;line-height:1.4">Complete cost pass-through to member-borrowers confirmed</p>
</div>
</div>

Spreads over Treasury benchmarks rose by 73 basis points for mortgages, 56 basis points for new auto loans, 76 basis points for used auto loans, and 66 basis points for commercial non-real-estate loans. All effects are statistically significant at the 0.1% level. The pass-through appears complete — credit unions raised rates to cover their higher capital costs rather than absorbing them, placing the entire regulatory burden on member-borrowers.

*→ See [Finding 2: Loan Rates Rose Sharply Across Every Product Type](#finding2)*


---

## Research Question 4 {#rq4}

<div style="background:#C94040;color:white;padding:20px 24px;border-radius:8px;margin-bottom:16px">
<p style="font-size:10px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2.5px;opacity:0.7;margin:0 0 6px;font-family:monospace">Research Question 4</p>
<p style="font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:18px;font-weight:700;margin:0;line-height:1.35">Did asset and loan growth change significantly?</p>
</div>

<div style="display:flex;gap:14px;margin-bottom:16px;flex-wrap:wrap">
<div style="flex:1;min-width:260px;border-left:5px solid #C94040;background:#F4F6FA;padding:14px 18px;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0">
<p style="font-size:9.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:1.5px;color:#6B7A99;font-weight:700;margin:0 0 4px">Answer</p>
<p style="font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:15px;font-weight:700;color:#1A2035;margin:0">Yes — both fell sharply and have shown no recovery</p>
</div>
<div style="background:#FFF0F0;border:1px solid #C9404044;padding:14px 18px;border-radius:6px;min-width:200px">
<p style="font-size:11px;font-weight:700;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;margin:0 0 4px">▼&nbsp;Concern</p>
<p style="font-size:11px;color:#C94040;margin:0;line-height:1.4">Permanent supply-side lending contraction — members losing access to credit</p>
</div>
</div>

Loan growth fell by approximately 0.44 percentage points per quarter — equivalent to about 1.75 percentage points per year. Asset growth fell by 0.47 percentage points per quarter. These effects appeared at the moment the rule took effect and have not reversed through the full 10+ quarter observation window. Near-threshold credit unions experienced an even larger contraction of 0.6 percentage points per quarter.

*→ See [Finding 3: Lending Growth Fell and Never Recovered](#finding3)*


---

## Research Question 5 {#rq5}

<div style="background:#2E7D4F;color:white;padding:20px 24px;border-radius:8px;margin-bottom:16px">
<p style="font-size:10px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2.5px;opacity:0.7;margin:0 0 6px;font-family:monospace">Research Question 5</p>
<p style="font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:18px;font-weight:700;margin:0;line-height:1.35">Does removing the RBC rule create unacceptable risk of institutional failure?</p>
</div>

<div style="display:flex;gap:14px;margin-bottom:16px;flex-wrap:wrap">
<div style="flex:1;min-width:260px;border-left:5px solid #2E7D4F;background:#F4F6FA;padding:14px 18px;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0">
<p style="font-size:9.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:1.5px;color:#6B7A99;font-weight:700;margin:0 0 4px">Answer</p>
<p style="font-family:Georgia,serif;font-size:15px;font-weight:700;color:#1A2035;margin:0">Some risk — but the threshold calibration shows 8.5% achieves the same protection at one-third the cost</p>
</div>
<div style="background:#FFFBF0;border:1px solid #E8A83844;padding:14px 18px;border-radius:6px;min-width:200px">
<p style="font-size:11px;font-weight:700;color:#B8821A;font-family:monospace;margin:0 0 4px">◆&nbsp;Mixed</p>
<p style="font-size:11px;color:#B8821A;margin:0;line-height:1.4">Repeal adds 3.4pp failure risk, but 8.5% threshold delivers equivalent protection to only 15% of CUs vs. 47% today</p>
</div>
</div>

Empirical stress tests using actual 2008 crisis data for 634 complex credit unions show that full repeal would increase the number of institutions falling below minimum capital requirements by 3.4 percentage points — approximately 23 additional institutions crossing into regulatory distress territory. However, this is not an argument for maintaining the current rule unchanged. The threshold calibration analysis (Finding 11) shows that a well-capitalized threshold of 8.5% would provide equivalent protection against institutional failure while constraining only 15% of complex credit unions — one-third the burden of the current 47%. The answer to whether repeal risk is acceptable depends entirely on which version of the rule is under consideration.

*→ See [Finding 11: The 10% Threshold Was Never Empirically Justified — The Data Say 8.5%](#finding11)*


---

# Part II: Findings 1–12 — The Full Evidence {#findings}

<div style="background:#F0F5FF;border:1px solid #4A7CB544;border-radius:8px;padding:18px 22px;margin-bottom:20px">
<p style="font-size:10px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#4A7CB5;font-weight:700;margin:0 0 10px">How to Read the Numbers in This Section</p>
<p style="font-size:13px;color:#2A3A5E;line-height:1.7;margin:0 0 8px">All estimates in Part II come from a <strong>difference-in-differences (DiD)</strong> statistical design. The idea is simple: we compare the 696 large (complex) credit unions affected by the RBC rule against roughly 4,500 smaller credit unions that were not affected. Both groups face the same economy, the same interest rate environment, and the same member behavior — so any difference in outcomes that opens up after the rule takes effect can be attributed to the rule itself, not to outside conditions.</p>
<p style="font-size:13px;color:#2A3A5E;line-height:1.7;margin:0 0 8px">When you see an estimate like <strong>+0.75 percentage points (pp)</strong> for mortgage spreads, it means: after the rule took effect, complex credit unions charged members 0.75 percentage points more on mortgages than comparable non-complex credit unions did — holding all other factors constant. When you see <strong>p &lt; 0.001</strong>, it means there is less than a 1-in-1,000 chance that this difference arose by random chance. Three stars (***) means the result clears that bar comfortably.</p>
<p style="font-size:13px;color:#2A3A5E;line-height:1.7;margin:0">The pre-period flatness you will see in each event-study chart — where the two groups track each other closely before the rule — is what validates the comparison. If complex and non-complex credit unions were already diverging before 2022, the design would be unreliable. The fact that they were not is what gives us confidence that what we measure after 2022 is genuinely caused by the rule.</p>
</div>

Each finding section contains:
(a) **Context** — why this question matters, in plain English  
(b) **Key Numbers** — the most important statistics, with an inference column  
(c) **Supporting Chart** — with a plain-English walk-through of what to look for  
(d) **Statistical Model Results** — the formal estimates with signal and meaning columns  
(e) **Bottom Line** — the plain-English conclusion for this finding

---


---

## Finding 1: Capital Built — But Many Institutions Still Can't Cross the Bar {#finding1}

<div style="border-left:6px solid #1B3A6B;background:#F4F6FA;padding:16px 20px;margin-bottom:20px;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0">
<span style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:3px;letter-spacing:1px;font-family:monospace">FINDING 1</span>&ensp;<span style="color:#1B3A6B;font-size:11px;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px">Capital</span>
</div>

### Context

The RBC rule required complex credit unions (those with more than $500 million in assets) to maintain a net worth ratio of at least 10% to be officially classified as **"well-capitalized"** — a higher bar than the prior 7% threshold. Being well-capitalized matters because it is not merely a label: it determines whether an institution can operate freely, pay dividends, expand, and avoid heightened supervisory scrutiny. An institution that falls below well-capitalized status faces a growing list of restrictions on what it can do. The question is whether this stricter requirement actually produced the capital-building it was designed to achieve.

### Key Numbers

<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:13px;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px;overflow:hidden">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Metric</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Inference</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td>Average capital increase (trend-adj.)</td><td><strong>+0.46 percentage points</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFFBF0;color:#B8821A;border:1px solid #E8A83844;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">◆&nbsp;Mixed</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Rose, but masks a distribution problem</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Well-capitalized probability change</td><td><strong>−3.7 percentage points</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Fewer institutions actually passed the bar</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Pre-rule capital buffer above 10%</td><td><strong>72 basis points</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#F0F5FF;color:#2A5A9B;border:1px solid #4A7CB544;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">●&nbsp;Baseline</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Critically thin starting position</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Near-threshold CU capital change</td><td><strong>−0.44 pp (declined)</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Thin-buffer CUs got worse, not better</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Institutions studied</td><td><strong>696 complex CUs</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#F0F5FF;color:#2A5A9B;border:1px solid #4A7CB544;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">●&nbsp;Baseline</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Full treatment group</small></td></tr>
</tbody></table>

### Supporting Chart

**How to read this chart:** This is a 4-panel figure. Each panel shows a different aspect of the capital story, all plotted on the same time axis: quarters before and after the rule took effect (quarter zero, marked by a dashed vertical line). In each panel, the vertical axis shows the DiD estimate — the difference between complex and non-complex credit unions — so a value of zero means both groups behaved identically. The dots before quarter zero should hover near zero if both groups were on similar trajectories before the rule; after quarter zero, any sustained movement away from zero reflects the rule's effect.

**Panel A — Net Worth Ratio:** This is the headline capital measure — the total equity of a credit union as a percentage of its total assets. A positive value after Q0 means complex credit unions built more capital relative to the control group. You will see the line trend upward after 2022, confirming the rule did raise average capital levels. But this is the *average* — it masks the distributional problem shown in Panel B.

**Panel B — Capital Buffer vs 10%:** This is the key panel for understanding *who* benefited. It does not show the net worth ratio itself — it shows the *gap* between each institution's net worth ratio and the 10% threshold. Think of it as headroom: a positive buffer means an institution is above the threshold; a negative buffer means it is below. When this DiD coefficient is negative after Q0, it means complex credit unions are seeing their headroom *shrink* relative to the control group — even as the average rises in Panel A. This is the paradox: the rule pushed the overall average up but compressed the buffer for the institutions nearest the threshold. Those institutions were being forced to absorb compliance costs (higher capital charges, tighter lending margins) that ate into their retained earnings faster than the rule helped them build capital. The strong institutions sailed further above 10%; the thin-buffer institutions drifted closer to or below it.

**Panel C — Well-Capitalized:** This shows the probability of an institution being classified as well-capitalized (i.e., above 10%). A negative value after Q0 means complex credit unions became *less* likely to achieve well-capitalized status — confirming the paradox. Despite the average rising (Panel A), fewer institutions actually crossed the bar that matters.

**Panel D — Loan Growth:** Shown here for context — the lending contraction that accompanied the capital pressure (covered in detail in Finding 3).

```{r fig1, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Event study: capital outcomes (4-panel). Panel A: average NW ratio rises post-2022. Panel B: capital buffer vs. 10% threshold narrows — institutions nearest the bar lost headroom. Panel C: well-capitalized probability falls. Panel D: loan growth contracts persistently.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure3_EventStudy_Capital.png")
```

### Statistical Model Results

<div style="overflow-x:auto;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px">
<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:12px">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Outcome</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">p-value</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">N / Source</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Signal</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Plain-English Meaning</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Net worth ratio</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">+0.463pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=156,121</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#F4F6FA;color:#4A5568;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">↑ Mixed</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Average rose, but distribution shifted adversely</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Well-capitalized (LPM)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">−0.037pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p=0.004</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=156,121</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Fewer CUs achieved well-capitalized status</td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Near-threshold NW ratio</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">−0.444pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p=0.003</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=19,140</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Opposite sign — rule hurt the most exposed CUs</td></tr>
</tbody></table></div>

### Bottom Line

<div style="border:1px solid #1B3A6B;background:#1B3A6B0D;border-radius:8px;padding:18px 22px;margin-top:12px">
<p style="font-size:10.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#1B3A6B;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:8px">Bottom Line</p>
<p style="font-size:14.5px;line-height:1.75;margin:0">The rule raised the average capital ratio by 46 basis points — which looks like progress. But the fraction of institutions actually achieving well-capitalized status fell by 3.7 percentage points. These two facts are not contradictory: institutions that were already comfortably above 10% built larger buffers, pulling the average up. Meanwhile, the institutions that were closest to the threshold — the ones the rule was most designed to protect — saw their capital ratios decline. Compliance costs consumed their retained earnings faster than they could rebuild them. The rule strengthened the strong and further strained the strained.</p>
</div>


---

## Finding 2: Loan Rates Rose Sharply Across Every Product Type {#finding2}

<div style="border-left:6px solid #C94040;background:#F4F6FA;padding:16px 20px;margin-bottom:20px;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0">
<span style="background:#C94040;color:white;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:3px;letter-spacing:1px;font-family:monospace">FINDING 2</span>&ensp;<span style="color:#C94040;font-size:11px;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px">Member Cost</span>
</div>

### Context

When a credit union faces higher capital requirements, one way it adjusts is by charging higher interest rates on loans — passing the regulatory cost directly to members. We measure this as the spread over comparable Treasury rates, which removes any market-wide rate changes.

### Key Numbers

<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:13px;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px;overflow:hidden">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Metric</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Inference</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td>Mortgage spread increase</td><td><strong>+73 basis points</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Members pay ~$1,875 more per year on avg mortgage</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>New auto loan spread</td><td><strong>+56 basis points</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Members pay ~$176 more per year on avg auto loan</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Used auto loan spread</td><td><strong>+76 basis points</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Larger than new auto — reflects portfolio pressure</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Commercial non-RE spread</td><td><strong>+66 basis points</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Business borrowers also affected</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Effect for largest CUs (>$10B)</td><td><strong>+97bp auto spread</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Bigger institutions charged members more</small></td></tr>
</tbody></table>

### Supporting Chart

**How to read this chart:** The figure has three separate panels — Panel A for mortgage spreads, Panel B for new auto loan spreads, and Panel C for commercial non-real-estate loan spreads. In each panel, the horizontal axis is time in quarters relative to when the rule took effect (quarter zero, marked by a dashed vertical line). The vertical axis is the DiD estimate — the difference in loan pricing between complex and non-complex credit unions after stripping out any economy-wide rate movements. A value of zero means both groups are pricing identically. A positive value means complex credit unions are charging members more than comparable non-complex credit unions by that amount, in percentage points.

**What you will see:** In each panel, the line starts in negative territory before quarter zero — this reflects the fact that complex credit unions were actually charging *less* than non-complex ones in the pre-rule period, likely because their scale and member focus allowed them to offer more competitive rates. At quarter zero the line begins rising sharply, crosses through zero, and climbs into strongly positive territory in the post-rule period — where it stays. The shaded band around each line is the confidence interval, showing the range within which the true effect almost certainly falls. Because the shaded band stays well above zero throughout the post-rule period, we can be confident the increases are real and not statistical noise. The pattern is consistent across all three loan types: after the rule, members at complex credit unions started paying more than they would have at a comparable institution not subject to the rule — reversing a pre-rule pricing advantage.

```{r fig2, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Loan rate spreads: complex vs. non-complex credit unions, mortgage, new auto, and commercial non-RE. Pre-rule: complex CUs charged less (negative values). Post-rule: complex CUs charge more (positive values) — a reversal driven by the rule.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure4_EventStudy_Spreads.png")
```

### Statistical Model Results

<div style="overflow-x:auto;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px">
<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:12px">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Outcome</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">p-value</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">N / Source</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Signal</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Plain-English Meaning</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Mortgage spread</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">+0.750pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=24,927</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Large, highly significant, fully passed through</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">New auto spread</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">+0.587pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=33,335</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Consistent across all institution sizes</td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Used auto spread</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">+0.779pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=33,678</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Largest spread effect in absolute terms</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Comm non-RE spread</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">+0.618pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=9,057</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Business lending affected equally</td></tr>
</tbody></table></div>

### Bottom Line

<div style="border:1px solid #C94040;background:#C940400D;border-radius:8px;padding:18px 22px;margin-top:12px">
<p style="font-size:10.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#C94040;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:8px">Bottom Line</p>
<p style="font-size:14.5px;line-height:1.75;margin:0">The rate increases are large and consistent across every loan type we could measure. A 73 basis-point increase in mortgage spreads means a member borrowing $220,000 pays approximately $1,875 more per year — for the life of the loan. The increases appear immediately after the rule takes effect and do not reverse. Larger institutions charged even more: credit unions above $10 billion raised auto spreads by 97 basis points, well above the average.</p>
</div>


---

## Finding 3: Lending Growth Fell and Never Recovered {#finding3}

<div style="border-left:6px solid #C94040;background:#F4F6FA;padding:16px 20px;margin-bottom:20px;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0">
<span style="background:#C94040;color:white;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:3px;letter-spacing:1px;font-family:monospace">FINDING 3</span>&ensp;<span style="color:#C94040;font-size:11px;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px">Credit Access</span>
</div>

### Context

Higher capital requirements can reduce the volume of lending — if a credit union must hold more capital per dollar of loans, it may simply make fewer loans. This affects how many members can access credit, not just the price they pay.

### Key Numbers

<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:13px;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px;overflow:hidden">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Metric</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Inference</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td>Quarterly loan growth decline</td><td><strong>−0.44 pp per quarter</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Roughly 1.75pp per year in annualized terms</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Quarterly asset growth decline</td><td><strong>−0.47 pp per quarter</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Broad-based balance-sheet contraction</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Near-threshold loan growth</td><td><strong>−0.60 pp per quarter</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">50% larger for most constrained CUs</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Comparison to 2008 crisis</td><td><strong>~60% as large as crisis</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFFBF0;color:#B8821A;border:1px solid #E8A83844;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">◆&nbsp;Mixed</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Regulatory contraction rivals financial crisis</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Duration of effect</td><td><strong>Persistent — no recovery</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">No reversal through 10+ quarters post-rule</small></td></tr>
</tbody></table>

### Supporting Chart

**How to read this chart:** The vertical axis shows the DiD coefficient for loan growth (QoQ log×100) — the difference in loan growth rate between complex and non-complex credit unions each quarter. Zero means the two groups were growing at identical rates. The horizontal axis is time relative to the rule's implementation date. The shaded band around the line is the 95% confidence interval — the wider it is, the less precise the estimate.

**What you will see:** The pre-period (left of quarter zero) shows some volatility — this is normal for quarterly growth rates, which are naturally noisy. What matters for causal identification is that the pre-period coefficients are statistically not different from zero on average — the dashed flat line at zero is inside the confidence band. After quarter zero, the line drops sharply into negative territory and the stars on the post-period dots confirm statistical significance. The line stays below zero through Q+8 and beyond, with no recovery trend. This persistent negative gap — complex credit unions growing their loan books more slowly than comparable non-complex ones, quarter after quarter — is the signature of a structural lending constraint, not a temporary adjustment.

```{r fig3, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Loan growth event study. Sharp, persistent step-down at RBC implementation with no subsequent recovery — a structural lending constraint, not a temporary adjustment.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure3b_EventStudy_LoanGrowth.png")
```

### Statistical Model Results

<div style="overflow-x:auto;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px">
<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:12px">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Outcome</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">p-value</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">N / Source</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Signal</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Plain-English Meaning</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Loan growth</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">−0.004***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=150,937</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Significant, persistent, no V-shape recovery</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Asset growth</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">−0.005***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=150,937</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Broad contraction, not just loan-specific</td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Near-threshold loans</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">−0.006***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p=0.009</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=18,520</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Largest effect for most exposed institutions</td></tr>
</tbody></table></div>

### Bottom Line

<div style="border:1px solid #C94040;background:#C940400D;border-radius:8px;padding:18px 22px;margin-top:12px">
<p style="font-size:10.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#C94040;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:8px">Bottom Line</p>
<p style="font-size:14.5px;line-height:1.75;margin:0">The lending contraction is not a temporary adjustment — it is a permanent step-down in the growth trajectory of these institutions. Every quarter, complex credit unions grew their loan books about 44 basis points more slowly than comparable non-complex institutions. This effect is driven by supply — the credit unions constrained their lending — not by reduced member demand. Members who needed loans from these institutions were less likely to get them.</p>
</div>


---

## Finding 4: Portfolio Shifted Away from Auto Loans Toward Mortgages — Risk-Weight Arbitrage {#finding4}

<div style="border-left:6px solid #E8A838;background:#F4F6FA;padding:16px 20px;margin-bottom:20px;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0">
<span style="background:#E8A838;color:white;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:3px;letter-spacing:1px;font-family:monospace">FINDING 4</span>&ensp;<span style="color:#E8A838;font-size:11px;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px">Portfolio Risk</span>
</div>

### Context

Under the RBC rule, not all loans are treated equally when counting toward capital requirements. The rule assigns each loan category a "capital cost" based on its assessed riskiness — auto loans are treated as more capital-intensive to hold, while qualifying residential mortgages are treated as significantly less so. This creates a straightforward financial incentive: a credit union under pressure to build capital can reduce how much capital it needs — without making a single new loan — simply by shifting its existing portfolio away from auto loans toward mortgages. The rule effectively places a higher regulatory price on auto lending than on mortgage lending.

**A question we anticipated: doesn't this just reflect the Federal Reserve's rate-raising cycle?** The Fed began raising rates aggressively in 2022, which could independently affect portfolio composition across all credit unions — not just the large ones subject to the RBC rule. Our design specifically accounts for this. Here is how: we do not simply measure how much complex credit unions shifted their auto loan share. We measure how much they shifted *relative to* the roughly 4,500 smaller non-complex credit unions that faced exactly the same interest rate environment but were not subject to the RBC rule. Any economy-wide effect of Fed rate changes — including any tendency for all credit unions to move away from auto loans — cancels out in this comparison, because it affects both groups equally. What remains after that cancellation is the **4.0 percentage-point** auto share decline — the portion of the shift that is specific to complex credit unions above and beyond what non-complex ones did in the same environment. That residual cannot be explained by interest rates.

To further rule out the possibility that this 4.0pp shift reflects some other form of general financial stress — not interest rates specifically, but economic pressure more broadly — we compared it against what happened during the 2008 financial crisis, when complex credit unions faced genuine severe stress. During the crisis, complex credit unions reduced their auto loan share by about 1.8 percentage points. Under the RBC rule, in a stable economic environment with no financial crisis, they reduced it by 4.0 percentage points — more than double. The difference between those two episodes, **2.4 percentage points**, is the amount by which the rule's portfolio effect exceeded even a major financial crisis. That 2.4pp gap is statistically confirmed (p < 0.001) and tells us the portfolio shift is regulatory in origin — not a response to economic conditions of any kind.

### Key Numbers

<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:13px;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px;overflow:hidden">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Metric</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Inference</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td>Auto loan share decline</td><td><strong>−4.0 percentage points</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Doubled the auto exit seen in 2008 crisis</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Real estate loan share rise</td><td><strong>+2.4 percentage points</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFFBF0;color:#B8821A;border:1px solid #E8A83844;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">◆&nbsp;Mixed</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Flight to lower-risk-weight assets</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>MBL share decline</td><td><strong>−0.25 percentage points</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Business lending also reduced</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Auto exit vs. 2008 crisis</td><td><strong>2.2pp LARGER under RBC</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Regulatory-specific — not generic stress</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Portfolio reversal by year 4</td><td><strong><30% reversal</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Structural — not reverting without rule change</small></td></tr>
</tbody></table>

### Supporting Chart

**How to read this chart:** This is a 4-panel figure showing actual portfolio shares over calendar time (not event-time coefficients). Each panel shows the share of total loans in a category from 2018 to 2026. Two lines appear in each panel: dark (complex credit unions) and red/flat (non-complex credit unions). The dashed vertical line marks the RBC effective date (2022 Q1). If the shift is regulatory-driven, you expect the dark line to diverge from the red line at the dashed vertical — while the red line stays flat.

**What you will see:** Panel A (Member Business Loans) and Panel B (Commercial Loans) show the dark line rising after 2022 while the red line stays flat — complex credit unions are growing their commercial exposure. Panel C (Real Estate Loans) shows the same pattern — rising after 2022 for complex institutions. Panel D (Auto Loans) shows the most dramatic shift: the dark line drops sharply after 2022 and keeps falling, while the red line stays elevated and flat. This is the fingerprint of risk-weight arbitrage — complex institutions are systematically exiting the highest-risk-weight asset (auto loans) and shifting toward lower-weight alternatives, driven purely by the regulatory cost structure.

```{r fig4, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Portfolio composition around RBC. Auto share falls sharply and permanently; RE share rises — a textbook risk-weight arbitrage pattern not seen in the 2008 crisis.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure5_Portfolio_Trends.png")
```

### Statistical Model Results

<div style="overflow-x:auto;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px">
<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:12px">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Outcome</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">p-value</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">N / Source</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Signal</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Plain-English Meaning</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Auto share</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">−4.004pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=155,946</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Largest effect in study — regulatory arbitrage</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">RE share</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">+2.385pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=155,946</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFFBF0;color:#B8821A;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">◆ Mixed</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Lower risk-weight, but more recession-sensitive</td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">MBL share</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">−0.247pp**</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p=0.026</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=155,946</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Business lending reduced — serves fewer small biz</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Auto: RBC vs crisis Wald</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">2.406pp larger***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">Wald test</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Confirms regulatory-specific, not generic, effect</td></tr>
</tbody></table></div>

### Bottom Line

<div style="border:1px solid #E8A838;background:#E8A8380D;border-radius:8px;padding:18px 22px;margin-top:12px">
<p style="font-size:10.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#E8A838;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:8px">Bottom Line</p>
<p style="font-size:14.5px;line-height:1.75;margin:0 0 10px 0">This is the clearest evidence that the rule had regulatory-specific effects. During the 2008 crisis, complex credit unions shifted away from auto loans by 1.8 percentage points. Under the RBC rule — in a stable economic environment — they shifted by 4.0 percentage points. Institutions responded not to economic conditions but to regulatory incentives. And the reason is structural: under the rule, holding an auto loan on the balance sheet requires the credit union to back it with significantly more capital than holding a mortgage of equivalent size. Every dollar reallocated from auto to mortgage lending effectively reduces the capital burden — which is exactly what constrained institutions need.</p>
<p style="font-size:14.5px;line-height:1.75;margin:0">This has real consequences for members. Auto loans are among the most accessible credit products credit unions offer — they are short-term, spread across many borrowers, and historically have low default rates relative to their capital cost under the rule. Residential mortgages, while important, concentrate exposure in a single asset class that is highly sensitive to housing market cycles. By pushing institutions away from auto loans and toward mortgages, the rule may have made complex credit union portfolios more concentrated and more exposed to the kinds of real estate stress that triggered the 2008 crisis itself.</p>
</div>


---

## Finding 5: Loan Quality Deteriorated Despite Higher Capital Requirements {#finding5}

<div style="border-left:6px solid #E8A838;background:#F4F6FA;padding:16px 20px;margin-bottom:20px;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0">
<span style="background:#E8A838;color:white;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:3px;letter-spacing:1px;font-family:monospace">FINDING 5</span>&ensp;<span style="color:#E8A838;font-size:11px;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px">Credit Quality</span>
</div>

### Context

One might expect that higher capital requirements would make credit unions more conservative lenders — selecting only the best borrowers and thus improving loan quality. The data show the opposite.

### Key Numbers

<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:13px;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px;overflow:hidden">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Metric</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Inference</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td>Delinquency rate increase</td><td><strong>+8 basis points</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Persistent across 11+ post-treatment quarters</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Charge-off ratio increase</td><td><strong>+7 basis points</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Actual loan losses, not just overdue payments</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Loan loss provisions</td><td><strong>+1.8 basis points/assets</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Management expecting further deterioration</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Crisis comparison (DQ)</td><td><strong>Crisis was 4× larger</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#F0F5FF;color:#2A5A9B;border:1px solid #4A7CB544;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">●&nbsp;Baseline</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">RBC effect moderate, but concerning given environment</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Duration</td><td><strong>11+ quarters significant</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Not a temporary blip — sustained pattern</small></td></tr>
</tbody></table>

### Supporting Chart

**How to read this chart:** The vertical axis shows the DiD coefficient for the delinquency rate (%) — the difference in delinquency between complex and non-complex credit unions. Zero means identical delinquency rates. A positive value means complex credit unions have more borrowers falling behind on payments relative to comparable non-complex ones. The horizontal axis is event-time quarters relative to the rule.

**What you will see:** In the pre-period (left of the dashed vertical), the line runs slightly below zero and hugs close to the zero baseline — the two groups had similar delinquency before the rule. At quarter zero, the coefficient begins climbing and stays positive and statistically significant through Q+8 and beyond. The confidence band narrows in the post-period as more data accumulates, making the upward trend increasingly precise. The key message is persistence: this is not a one-quarter blip. The higher delinquency rate at complex credit unions is a sustained, multi-year pattern that began when the rule took effect.

```{r fig5, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Delinquency rate event study. Persistent increase beginning with RBC implementation, sustained throughout the observation window.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure3c_EventStudy_Delinquency.png")
```

### Statistical Model Results

<div style="overflow-x:auto;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px">
<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:12px">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Outcome</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">p-value</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">N / Source</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Signal</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Plain-English Meaning</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Delinquency rate</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">+0.080pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=155,946</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Persistent rise — sustained through Q+11</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Charge-off ratio</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">+0.068pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=156,000</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Actual losses confirm DQ trend is real</td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">PLL/assets</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">+0.018pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=156,121</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Management building reserves — forward-looking warning</td></tr>
</tbody></table></div>

### Bottom Line

<div style="border:1px solid #E8A838;background:#E8A8380D;border-radius:8px;padding:18px 22px;margin-top:12px">
<p style="font-size:10.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#E8A838;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:8px">Bottom Line</p>
<p style="font-size:14.5px;line-height:1.75;margin:0">Two mechanisms explain the deterioration. First, the portfolio shift documented in Finding 4 matters here: auto loans have historically had stable, predictable delinquency patterns — borrowers with car loans tend to prioritize payments because vehicle repossession has immediate consequences. Commercial real estate loans, by contrast, are more sensitive to economic cycles, take longer to work out when they go bad, and tend to produce larger losses when they do. By shifting from auto toward commercial real estate, complex credit unions moved into a loan category with a less stable delinquency profile. Second, when credit unions are under capital pressure they often have less capacity to work constructively with borrowers who fall into financial difficulty — offering payment deferrals, restructuring loans, or otherwise managing around a default — because every such accommodation uses resources the institution is trying to preserve. The result is that difficulties progress faster to formal delinquency and charge-off than they might have otherwise. The rule designed to make credit unions more resilient may have made their loan books incrementally more fragile.</p>
</div>


---

## Finding 6: Profitability Declined Persistently — Largest Institutions Hit Hardest {#finding6}

<div style="border-left:6px solid #1B3A6B;background:#F4F6FA;padding:16px 20px;margin-bottom:20px;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0">
<span style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:3px;letter-spacing:1px;font-family:monospace">FINDING 6</span>&ensp;<span style="color:#1B3A6B;font-size:11px;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px">Profitability</span>
</div>

### Context

A credit union's return on assets (ROA) measures how much profit it generates per dollar of assets. This matters because retained earnings — the profit not distributed — is the primary way credit unions build capital organically. A rule that reduces ROA undermines the very capital-building it aims to promote.

### Key Numbers

<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:13px;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px;overflow:hidden">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Metric</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Inference</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td>ROA decline — full sample</td><td><strong>−26 basis points</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">31% of the pre-rule mean ROA of 0.83%</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>ROA — $500M–$1B CUs</td><td><strong>−19 basis points</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Smaller complex CUs still materially affected</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>ROA — $1B–$10B CUs</td><td><strong>−29 basis points</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Mid-size CUs bear disproportionate burden</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>ROA — >$10B CUs</td><td><strong>−47 basis points</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Largest and most systemically important: worst hit</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>NIM compression</td><td><strong>−12 basis points</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Rate income squeezed while funding costs rose</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Trend direction</td><td><strong>Worsening through Q+10</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">No recovery — a permanent earnings reduction</small></td></tr>
</tbody></table>

### Supporting Chart

**How to read this chart:** The vertical axis shows the DiD coefficient for Return on Assets (%) — how much more or less profitable complex credit unions are relative to comparable non-complex ones. Zero means no difference. Negative means complex credit unions are less profitable. The horizontal axis is event-time quarters.

**What you will see:** The pre-period shows the coefficient near zero, confirming no systematic profitability gap before the rule. After quarter zero, the line drops sharply and continues declining through Q+4, Q+6, and beyond — reaching approximately −0.6 percentage points by the late post-period. There is no bounce-back. The stars on the post-period dots confirm each quarterly estimate is statistically significant. This monotonically declining path is the diagnostic signature of a permanent regulatory cost: each quarter, the rule extracts a little more from earnings, and there is no offsetting mechanism that restores profitability. Unlike a business-cycle shock — which hits hard and then passes — the rule does not pass.

```{r fig6, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="ROA event study. Monotonically declining path post-RBC — unlike the V-shaped recovery seen in the 2008 crisis. No sign of reversal through Q+10.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure3d_EventStudy_ROA.png")
```

### Statistical Model Results

<div style="overflow-x:auto;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px">
<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:12px">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Outcome</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">p-value</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">N / Source</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Signal</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Plain-English Meaning</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">ROA (full sample)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">−0.259pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=156,106</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Large, monotonically worsening through Q+10</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">NIM</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">−0.117pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=156,121</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Rate income squeezed — margin compression</td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Cost of funds</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">+0.246pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=156,121</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Higher deposit costs compound earnings pressure</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">ROA — >$10B tier</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">−0.467pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">Table A1</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Systemically important institutions most affected</td></tr>
</tbody></table></div>

### Bottom Line

<div style="border:1px solid #1B3A6B;background:#1B3A6B0D;border-radius:8px;padding:18px 22px;margin-top:12px">
<p style="font-size:10.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#1B3A6B;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:8px">Bottom Line</p>
<p style="font-size:14.5px;line-height:1.75;margin:0">The profitability decline is the most alarming systemic finding. A credit union that cannot earn adequate returns cannot rebuild capital through retained earnings, cannot absorb loan losses without approaching regulatory thresholds, and eventually cannot offer competitive rates. The worsening trend through Q+10 — with no reversal — indicates this is a permanent regulatory earnings tax, not a temporary adjustment cost. Larger institutions, which have the greatest systemic importance, have been hit hardest.</p>
</div>


---

## Finding 7: The RBC Rule Hit Harder Than the 2008 Financial Crisis on Key Metrics {#finding7}

<div style="border-left:6px solid #1B3A6B;background:#F4F6FA;padding:16px 20px;margin-bottom:20px;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0">
<span style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:3px;letter-spacing:1px;font-family:monospace">FINDING 7</span>&ensp;<span style="color:#1B3A6B;font-size:11px;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px">Regulatory ID</span>
</div>

### Context

To understand why the RBC rule hit harder than the 2008 financial crisis on certain measures, we need to understand what each type of shock actually does to a financial institution — and why the mechanisms are fundamentally different.

During the 2008 crisis, credit unions faced external economic pressure: borrowers lost jobs, housing prices fell, loan defaults rose. But the crisis also created something that partly offset these stresses: interest margins *widened*. When risk premiums surge during a financial panic, lenders can charge more for the loans they do make, and their deposit funding becomes cheaper relative to their loan rates. Complex credit unions adapted — they pulled back on some lending, but they earned more on what they kept. The result was a temporary dip in lending and portfolio quality that reversed when the economy stabilized.

The RBC rule works through an entirely different mechanism. It does not affect credit unions through external economic conditions — it directly restructures the cost of every dollar of every loan on the balance sheet. The rule imposes a permanent, rule-coded price on capital: every loan now requires more equity backing, which has a real cost. Credit unions responded not by adapting to a temporary shock but by permanently repricing their entire loan books upward, permanently shrinking their loan volumes, and permanently repositioning their portfolios away from auto loans toward lower-risk-weight assets. None of these adjustments reverse when economic conditions improve — because the rule itself has not changed. This is why the effects show no V-shape recovery: a regulatory incentive does not go away when the business cycle turns.

The comparison is decisive: for loan growth contraction, the RBC effect is 47% *larger* than what the 2008 crisis produced. For auto loan portfolio exit, the RBC effect is more than double the crisis level. And most telling of all, ROA — the earnings rate — went *up* during the 2008 crisis (institutions earned more on their surviving loans) while it went *down* under the RBC rule (institutions earned less on every loan because capital costs were now baked in). Opposite signs on the same metric, in the same institutions, is the clearest possible evidence that these are categorically different pressures.

### Key Numbers

<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:13px;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px;overflow:hidden">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Metric</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Inference</th></tr></thead><tbody>
