<tr><td>Loan growth contraction: RBC vs. crisis</td><td><strong>RBC 47% larger</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Rule suppressed lending more than the financial crisis</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Auto exit: RBC vs. crisis</td><td><strong>+2.4pp larger under RBC</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Risk-weight arbitrage — regulatory-specific</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>ROA sign under crisis</td><td><strong>+7bp (flat/positive)</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#F0F5FF;color:#2A5A9B;border:1px solid #4A7CB544;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">●&nbsp;Baseline</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Crisis did not compress earnings at complex CUs</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>ROA sign under RBC</td><td><strong>−26bp (persistent decline)</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Opposite sign — rule is a permanent earnings tax</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>NIM under crisis</td><td><strong>+23bp (widened)</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#F0FFF5;color:#2E7D4F;border:1px solid #2E7D4F44;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▲&nbsp;Positive</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Crisis widened spreads — institutions adapted</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>NIM under RBC</td><td><strong>−12bp (compressed)</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Rule compressed income — opposite to crisis</small></td></tr>
</tbody></table>

### Supporting Chart

**How to read this chart:** This is a 4-panel figure showing two overlapping event studies per panel — red triangles for the 2008 crisis episode and navy circles for the 2022 RBC episode. The x-axis in each panel is time measured from the onset of each episode (Q0 = 2008Q3 for crisis, Q0 = 2022Q1 for RBC). The four panels show: A. Net Worth Ratio, B. Capital Buffer (vs 10% threshold), C. Loan Growth, D. Delinquency Rate. Stars mark statistically significant quarters.

**What you will see:** Panels A and B (capital outcomes) show similar patterns in both episodes — capital dips during the crisis and rises under the RBC rule, which is expected in both cases. The critical divergences are in Panels C and D. For loan growth (Panel C), the RBC navy line drops sharply at Q0 and stays depressed with no recovery; the crisis red line also drops but begins recovering by Q+4. For delinquency (Panel D), the crisis red line stays flat-to-positive and recovers, while the RBC navy line trends upward through the post-period. These opposite-sign patterns on the same institutions across two episodes confirm the regulatory channel: the rule is doing something to these institutions that a financial crisis did not.

```{r fig7, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Parallel DiD comparing 2008 crisis vs. 2022 RBC rule. Capital responses are similar; profitability and portfolio show opposite-sign patterns — confirming a regulatory channel.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure6_Crisis_vs_RBC_Capital.png")
```

### Statistical Model Results

<div style="overflow-x:auto;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px">
<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:12px">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Outcome</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">p-value</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">N / Source</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Signal</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Plain-English Meaning</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Loan growth diff (RBC larger)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">+0.008pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p=0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">Wald test</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">RBC suppressed lending significantly more than crisis</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Auto share diff (RBC larger)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">−2.406pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">Wald test</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Regulatory arbitrage confirmed — not generic stress</td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">ROA diff (RBC worse)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">−0.487pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">Wald test</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Opposite signs — rule is a regulatory earnings tax</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">NIM diff (RBC worse)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">−0.403pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p<0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">Wald test</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Confirms margin compression is regulatory-specific</td></tr>
</tbody></table></div>

### Bottom Line

<div style="border:1px solid #1B3A6B;background:#1B3A6B0D;border-radius:8px;padding:18px 22px;margin-top:12px">
<p style="font-size:10.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#1B3A6B;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:8px">Bottom Line</p>
<p style="font-size:14.5px;line-height:1.75;margin:0">During the 2008 financial crisis — the worst economic shock in post-war history — complex credit unions saw flat-to-positive ROA, because they could charge higher rates and credit losses were offset by earnings. Under the RBC rule — in a stable economic environment — ROA declined persistently and NIM compressed. This opposite-sign pattern for profitability is the clearest evidence that the RBC rule functions as a regulatory earnings tax rather than a genuine safety measure. The rule is doing something the financial crisis did not.</p>
</div>


---

## Finding 8: Auto Loan Exit Is Permanent Under RBC — Unlike Every Other Crisis Response {#finding8}

<div style="border-left:6px solid #E8A838;background:#F4F6FA;padding:16px 20px;margin-bottom:20px;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0">
<span style="background:#E8A838;color:white;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:3px;letter-spacing:1px;font-family:monospace">FINDING 8</span>&ensp;<span style="color:#E8A838;font-size:11px;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px">Structural Change</span>
</div>

### Context

When credit unions face stress, they adjust their portfolios. The critical question is whether this adjustment is temporary (they snap back when pressure eases) or permanent (structural). The answer depends on what drove the adjustment — economic conditions reverse; regulatory incentives don't.

### Key Numbers

<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:13px;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px;overflow:hidden">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Metric</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Inference</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td>Auto share path under crisis</td><td><strong>V-shaped — fell then recovered</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#F0FFF5;color:#2E7D4F;border:1px solid #2E7D4F44;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▲&nbsp;Positive</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Classic temporary stress response</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Auto share path under RBC</td><td><strong>Step-change — fell and stayed</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Structural — regulatory incentive permanent</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>ROA path under crisis</td><td><strong>V-shaped — declined then recovered</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#F0FFF5;color:#2E7D4F;border:1px solid #2E7D4F44;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▲&nbsp;Positive</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Normal recovery once shock passed</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>ROA path under RBC</td><td><strong>Linear decline through Q+10</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">No recovery — permanent regulatory tax</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Capital path: both episodes</td><td><strong>V-shaped in both</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#F0F5FF;color:#2A5A9B;border:1px solid #4A7CB544;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">●&nbsp;Baseline</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Generic response — expected in both cases</small></td></tr>
</tbody></table>

### Supporting Chart

**How to read this chart:** Another 4-panel comparison figure — red triangles for the 2008 crisis, navy circles for the 2022 RBC rule. The four panels cover: A. Real Estate Share, B. Auto Loan Share, C. MBL Share, D. ROA. Each panel shows how each outcome evolved in the two episodes relative to Q0.

**What you will see:** Panel B (Auto Loan Share) is the most striking. Under the 2008 crisis (red), auto loan share dips briefly and climbs back — a clear V-shape, fully reversing within about 10 quarters. Under the RBC rule (navy), the auto loan share drops at implementation and continues falling, with no reversal visible even after many quarters. Panel D (ROA) shows the opposite-sign result described in Finding 7: the crisis red line wobbles near zero and recovers; the RBC navy line slopes steadily downward. Panel A (Real Estate Share) shows the mirror of Panel B — under the RBC rule, institutions are shifting into real estate at the same time they are exiting auto loans, confirming the risk-weight arbitrage story. This four-panel view makes the structural vs. temporary distinction visually undeniable.

```{r fig8, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Shape comparison: V-shapes under crisis, step-changes under RBC. The divergence in portfolio and profitability paths is the structural signature of regulatory-specific effects.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure6b_Crisis_vs_RBC_Portfolio.png")
```

### Statistical Model Results

**What does ">90% at Q+10" mean?** When the rule took effect, complex credit unions reduced their auto loan share by about 4 percentage points. "Q+10" means ten quarters (two and a half years) after the rule started. ">90% at Q+10" means that at that point, more than 90% of the original auto loan decline was still present — meaning the exit had barely reversed at all. By contrast, during the 2008 crisis, the auto share also fell — but by Q+10 after the crisis, less than 30% of that decline remained. Nearly all of it had reversed. The contrast between ">90% still there" (rule) and "<30% still there" (crisis) is the quantitative definition of structural versus temporary.

<div style="overflow-x:auto;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px">
<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:12px">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Outcome</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">What the Data Show</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Signal</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Plain-English Meaning</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Auto share exit — still present at Q+10 (RBC)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B">&gt;90% of exit remains</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">More than 9 out of 10 percentage points of the original decline are still there 2.5 years later — the exit is structural, not temporary</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Auto share exit — still present at Q+10 (2008 crisis)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B">&lt;30% of exit remains</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#F0FFF5;color:#2E7D4F;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▲ Positive</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Crisis-era auto loan exit mostly reversed within 2 years — a normal, temporary response to stress</td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">ROA recovery path (2008 crisis)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B">Back to baseline by Q+8</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#F0FFF5;color:#2E7D4F;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▲ Positive</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Profitability recovered within 2 years after the crisis ended — V-shaped, as expected</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">ROA trajectory (RBC rule)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B">No reversal through Q+10</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Profitability keeps declining with no floor in sight — a permanent earnings reduction, not a temporary dip</td></tr>
</tbody></table></div>

### Bottom Line

<div style="border:1px solid #E8A838;background:#E8A8380D;border-radius:8px;padding:18px 22px;margin-top:12px">
<p style="font-size:10.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#E8A838;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:8px">Bottom Line</p>
<p style="font-size:14.5px;line-height:1.75;margin:0">V-shaped responses are what we expect from temporary shocks. Step-change responses indicate structural realignment driven by permanent incentives. The auto loan exit under the RBC rule is structural because the risk-weight incentive never changes — unless the rule is redesigned. Complex credit unions have permanently repositioned their portfolios away from auto loans, regardless of whether this is good for their members or their own financial health.</p>
</div>


---

## Finding 9: The CCULR Relief Valve Didn't Work {#finding9}

<div style="border-left:6px solid #4A7CB5;background:#F4F6FA;padding:16px 20px;margin-bottom:20px;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0">
<span style="background:#4A7CB5;color:white;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:3px;letter-spacing:1px;font-family:monospace">FINDING 9</span>&ensp;<span style="color:#4A7CB5;font-size:11px;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px">Design Flaw</span>
</div>

### Context

Recognizing that the 10% threshold might be too burdensome, NCUA created a simpler alternative — the Complex Credit Union Leverage Ratio (CCULR) — with a lower 9% threshold. This was designed to provide regulatory relief to constrained institutions. The data show it did not.

### Key Numbers

<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:13px;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px;overflow:hidden">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Metric</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Inference</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td>Loan growth relief from CCULR</td><td><strong>Not significant (p=0.197)</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Zero lending relief — the primary goal failed</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Capital outcome for CCULR adopters</td><td><strong>Worse than full RBC</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Adopters ended up with less capital</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>ROA for CCULR adopters</td><td><strong>−32bp vs −24bp (full RBC)</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Worse profitability — selection effect</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>Why it failed</td><td><strong>Distressed CUs self-selected</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Relief went to institutions that couldn't use it</small></td></tr>
<tr><td>1pp threshold reduction adequacy</td><td><strong>Insufficient for bound CUs</strong></td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">9% vs 10% doesn't help a CU at 8%</small></td></tr>
</tbody></table>

### Supporting Chart

**How to read this chart:** This is a 6-panel figure with three lines per panel: red triangles (full-RBC complex CUs), navy circles (CCULR adopters), and both measured relative to non-complex credit unions as a control. The six panels cover: A. Net Worth Ratio, B. Capital Buffer, C. Loan Growth, D. Delinquency Rate, E. ROA, F. Auto Loan Share. If CCULR were providing genuine relief, the navy CCULR line should sit between the red full-RBC line and the zero baseline — showing partial improvement.

**What you will see:** Two things stand out across the panels. First, in the pre-period (left of quarter zero), the navy CCULR line is already diverging downward from the red full-RBC line — CCULR adopters were already weaker before the rule took effect. This pre-trend violation tells us that CCULR adoption was not a free choice among equals; distressed institutions self-selected into it. Second, in the post-period, the navy line performs worse than the red line in panels C (loan growth), E (ROA), and F (auto share) — CCULR adopters have lower profitability, more lending contraction, and no portfolio relief compared to full-RBC institutions. The CCULR line falls below, not between, the other lines.

```{r fig9, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Three-group event study. CCULR adopters show pre-trend violations and worse outcomes — confirming negative selection rather than genuine relief.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure7_CCULR_EventStudy.png")
```

### Statistical Model Results

<div style="overflow-x:auto;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px">
<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:12px">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Outcome</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">p-value</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">N / Source</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Signal</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Plain-English Meaning</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Loan growth relief (β₂−β₁)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">−0.002 (n.s.)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p=0.197</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=~156K</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF8F0;color:#8B4513;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">✗ No effect</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">PRIMARY GOAL: zero significant relief achieved</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Capital relief</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">−0.374pp***</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p=0.004</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=~156K</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Worse</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">CCULR adopters have LESS capital than full RBC CUs</td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">ROA relief</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">−0.084pp**</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p=0.011</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=~156K</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Worse</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Profitability also worse — confirms selection</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Auto share relief</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">+0.136 (n.s.)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">p=0.817</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">N=~156K</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF8F0;color:#8B4513;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">✗ No effect</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Portfolio reallocation equally bad under CCULR</td></tr>
</tbody></table></div>

### Bottom Line

<div style="border:1px solid #4A7CB5;background:#4A7CB50D;border-radius:8px;padding:18px 22px;margin-top:12px">
<p style="font-size:10.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#4A7CB5;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:8px">Bottom Line</p>
<p style="font-size:14.5px;line-height:1.75;margin:0">The CCULR failed as a relief mechanism for a structural reason: the institutions that adopted it were disproportionately those already in financial difficulty. A credit union with an 8% capital ratio that adopts the 9% CCULR threshold has gained no regulatory breathing room — it is still below the threshold. The 1 percentage point reduction was simply not large enough to provide meaningful relief to genuinely constrained institutions.</p>
</div>


---

## Finding 10: Repeal Carries Real Risk — But the Magnitude Is Modest and Quantifiable {#finding10}

<div style="border-left:6px solid #C94040;background:#F4F6FA;padding:16px 20px;margin-bottom:20px;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0">
<span style="background:#C94040;color:white;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:3px;letter-spacing:1px;font-family:monospace">FINDING 10</span>&ensp;<span style="color:#C94040;font-size:11px;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px">Prudential Risk</span>
</div>

### Context

Everything above documents costs of the rule. But a rule designed to protect the financial system deserves a fair accounting of its actual protective value — not just an assumption that it must be working. This finding does exactly that: it quantifies the rule's protective benefit using empirical stress tests built from actual 2008 crisis data for 634 complex credit unions.

The methodology is deliberately conservative in the rule's favor. We take each institution's actual current capital position, apply the actual capital drawdowns we measured during the 2008 crisis (not assumptions — real data from NCUA Call Reports), and count how many would fall below the 7% adequacy floor under each capital regime. Three regimes are compared: pre-rule (what capital looked like before 2022), with-rule (current state), and post-repeal (what capital would look like if the rule were removed and positions reverted toward pre-rule levels). Three stress severities are tested: 2008-equivalent (the actual historical crisis), 1.5× (moderately worse), and 2.0× (an extreme scenario roughly twice as bad as 2008).

The central finding is nuanced and must be stated carefully: **repeal does add real institutional risk**, but the current rule provides almost no protection at historically realistic stress levels, because both the pre-rule and the with-rule failure rates are nearly identical at 2008-calibrated severity. The rule's protective value only becomes meaningful at extreme stress scenarios — roughly twice as severe as 2008 — that have no historical precedent.

### Key Numbers

<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:13px;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px;overflow:hidden">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Metric</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">What It Means in Plain English</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Crisis data: CUs studied</td><td style="padding:9px 13px"><strong>634 complex credit unions</strong></td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">These are the institutions that were both large enough to be subject to the RBC rule and also operating during the 2008 crisis — giving us actual historical data on how they performed under severe stress.</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Mean peak-to-trough capital loss</td><td style="padding:9px 13px"><strong>1.86 percentage points</strong></td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">On average, a complex credit union's net worth ratio fell by about 1.86 percentage points from its highest point before the crisis to its lowest point during the crisis. An institution at 11% capital before the crisis would have dropped to about 9.1% at the worst point.</td></tr>
<tr><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Worst-hit 10% of institutions</td><td style="padding:9px 13px"><strong>Lost 3.36pp or more</strong></td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">The 10% of institutions most severely affected by the crisis lost more than 3.36 percentage points of capital — nearly double the average. This is why we test each institution using its own historical drawdown rather than applying a single average to everyone.</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Rule's protection at 2008-level stress</td><td style="padding:9px 13px"><strong>−0.3pp (statistically zero)</strong></td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">When we apply actual 2008 drawdowns to each institution, the failure rate is 19.1% under pre-rule capital positions and 19.4% with the rule — a difference of just −0.3pp. Statistically, this is indistinguishable from zero. Three years of capital-building under the rule produced essentially no improvement in crisis survival at historical stress levels.</td></tr>
<tr><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Additional failures under repeal (2008 stress)</td><td style="padding:9px 13px"><strong>+3.4pp vs. with-rule</strong></td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">If the rule were removed and capital positions reverted toward pre-rule levels, approximately 3.4 percentage points more institutions would fall below the 7% floor in a 2008-severity crisis. With 674 complex credit unions in the post-rule sample, this translates to roughly 23 additional institutions in distress.</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Member welfare cost per institution protected</td><td style="padding:9px 13px"><strong>~$14B per institution</strong></td><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">The total 4-year member welfare cost of the rule is approximately $325.9 billion (see Part III). Dividing by the ~23 additional institutions the rule protects from distress in a 2008-severity scenario gives $325.9B ÷ 23 ≈ $14 billion per institution protected. This is the cost-benefit ratio the empirical evidence produces.</td></tr>
</tbody></table>

### Supporting Chart

**How to read this chart:** This is a grouped bar chart. Each cluster of bars represents one stress severity scenario (2008-equivalent, 1.5×, 2.0×). Within each cluster, there are four bars: pre-rule capital, with-rule capital, post-repeal at 4 quarters, and post-repeal at 8 quarters. The height of each bar is the fraction of the 634 complex credit unions whose capital ratio would fall below 7% — the regulatory floor that triggers serious supervisory action — after applying the stress drawdown to their actual capital positions.

**What you will see:** At the 2008-equivalent severity, the first two bars in the cluster (pre-rule and with-rule) are almost exactly the same height — approximately 19%. This is the most important comparison in the chart: it shows that the 3 years of capital-building driven by the RBC rule has produced essentially no improvement in failure rates at historical stress levels. Why? Because the institutions that were already well-capitalized stayed well above 7% under either regime, and the institutions that were near the threshold under the pre-rule regime are *still* near the threshold under the with-rule regime — because their retained earnings were consumed by compliance costs. The third and fourth bars (post-repeal) are taller at about 22–26% — this is the real risk cost of repeal. That 3–4 percentage point gap is approximately 20–25 institutions. Moving to the 2.0× severity cluster, the bars spread further apart — the rule provides more protection at extreme stress. This is the scenario where maintaining capital buffers matters most. But it is also the scenario with no historical precedent.

```{r fig10, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Stress survival rates by regime and severity. At 2008-severity the rule provides just −0.3pp of protection. Repeal adds 3.4pp of institutional failure risk.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure11_4_Survival_Frontier.png")
```

### Statistical Model Results

<div style="overflow-x:auto;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px">
<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:12px">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Outcome</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">p-value</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">N / Source</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Signal</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Plain-English Meaning</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Pre→With rule (2008 stress)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">−0.3pp</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">n.s.</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">634 CUs</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFFBF0;color:#B8821A;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">◆ Mixed</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Rule provides negligible protection at historical severity</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">With rule→Repeal (2008)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">+3.4pp</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">—</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">634 CUs</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Real but modest: ~23 additional institutions at risk</td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">With rule→Repeal (1.5× severity)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">+3.1pp</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">—</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">634 CUs</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Consistent finding across all stress severities</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">With rule→Repeal (2.0× severity)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">+4.6pp</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">—</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">634 CUs</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Rule more protective at extreme stress levels — but still costly</td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">4-yr member welfare cost</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B;white-space:nowrap">$325.9B</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">—</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99;white-space:nowrap">3D/3E</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px;white-space:nowrap">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">$14B per institution protected — disproportionate</td></tr>
</tbody></table></div>

### Bottom Line

<div style="border:1px solid #C94040;background:#C940400D;border-radius:8px;padding:18px 22px;margin-top:12px">
<p style="font-size:10.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#C94040;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:8px">Bottom Line</p>
<p style="font-size:14.5px;line-height:1.75;margin:0">Repeal is not without cost: approximately 23 additional complex credit unions would fall below the 7% adequacy floor in a 2008-severity crisis. This is a real concern and should not be dismissed. However, the empirical evidence establishes that the current rule provides essentially zero protection at historical stress levels — the −0.3pp difference between pre-rule and with-rule failure rates is statistically indistinguishable from zero. The rule's protective value only becomes meaningful at extreme scenarios far outside historical experience. The $325.9 billion in foregone member welfare — approximately $14 billion per institution protected — is a cost-benefit ratio that the data do not support under any conventional standard.<sup>†</sup></p>
</div>

<div style="background:#F8F9FC;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px;padding:14px 20px;margin-top:10px;font-size:11.5px;color:#4A5568;line-height:1.7">
<p style="font-size:10px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#9BA8C0;font-weight:700;margin:0 0 8px"><sup>†</sup> How the $325.9B and $14B figures are computed</p>
<p style="margin:0 0 6px"><strong>$325.9 billion</strong> is the cumulative 4-year member welfare gain from removing the rule under the central (Gradual) repeal scenario in Part III. It is calculated by taking the DiD-estimated loan spread increases (73bp for mortgages, 59bp for new auto, 78bp for used auto, 62bp for commercial non-RE), multiplying each by the total outstanding loan balances in that category across all 696 complex credit unions (~$750 billion combined), and accumulating the savings quarter by quarter as rates gradually recover over 16 quarters. The Gradual scenario assumes institutions return 60% of the spread increase within 2 years and the remainder by year 4.</p>
<p style="margin:0"><strong>$14 billion per institution</strong> is $325.9B divided by approximately 23 — the number of additional institutions that fall below the 7% adequacy floor in a 2008-severity stress test under repeal vs. the current rule. It represents the member welfare cost of maintaining the rule specifically to protect those 23 institutions from crisis-level distress.</p>
</div>


---

## Finding 11: The 10% Threshold Was Never Empirically Justified — The Data Say 8.5% {#finding11}

<div style="border-left:6px solid #2E7D4F;background:#F4F6FA;padding:16px 20px;margin-bottom:20px;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0">
<span style="background:#2E7D4F;color:white;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:3px;letter-spacing:1px;font-family:monospace">FINDING 11</span>&ensp;<span style="color:#2E7D4F;font-size:11px;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px">Threshold Calibration</span>
</div>

### Context — Why This Matters

This finding is the most operationally actionable result in the entire study. Every other finding documents what the rule costs and what it protects against. This finding answers the question that logically follows: *if we accept that some capital threshold is necessary, what should it be?*

The answer to that question was never derived empirically when the rule was designed. The 10% well-capitalized threshold was chosen by regulatory judgment — a round number set above the existing 7% legacy threshold to create a meaningful buffer. That is not a criticism; most capital rules are designed this way. But it means we do not actually know whether 10% is the right number. It could be set too high, imposing unnecessary costs on a large number of institutions without a commensurate gain in safety. Only a data-driven analysis can answer that.

What we can do is test every possible threshold level from 6.5% to 10.5% and ask two questions simultaneously: how many institutions would be constrained by a threshold at that level, and how many institutions that are *above* that threshold would still fail if a 2008-severity crisis struck? The first number rises as we raise the threshold — a higher bar constrains more institutions. The second falls — a higher bar means fewer unconstrained institutions are in risky territory. The point where these two curves cross is the threshold at which adding one more basis point of requirement starts costing more in compliance burden than it saves in failure prevention. That crossing point, derived entirely from actual Call Report data, is approximately **8.5%**.

### Key Numbers

<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:13px;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px;overflow:hidden">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Metric</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">At Current 10% Rule</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">At Recalibrated 8.5%</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Inference</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:10px 13px;font-weight:600">Fraction of complex CUs constrained</td><td style="padding:10px 13px;font-weight:700;color:#C94040">47% of 696 CUs</td><td style="padding:10px 13px;font-weight:700;color:#2E7D4F">~15% of 696 CUs</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:2px 8px;border-radius:3px;background:#F0FFF5;color:#2E7D4F;border:1px solid #2E7D4F44;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▲&nbsp;Positive</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">68% fewer institutions constrained unnecessarily</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:10px 13px;font-weight:600">Residual failure rate among unconstrained CUs (2008 stress)</td><td style="padding:10px 13px;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B">~0%</td><td style="padding:10px 13px;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B">~20%</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:2px 8px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFFBF0;color:#B8821A;border:1px solid #E8A83844;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">◆&nbsp;Mixed</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Higher residual risk — but equivalent to 10% protection level at crossing point</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:10px 13px;font-weight:600">Empirically optimal crossing point</td><td style="padding:10px 13px;font-weight:700;color:#C94040">Not at 10%</td><td style="padding:10px 13px;font-weight:700;color:#2E7D4F">~8.5%</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:2px 8px;border-radius:3px;background:#F0FFF5;color:#2E7D4F;border:1px solid #2E7D4F44;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▲&nbsp;Positive</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Where marginal burden = marginal protection — derived from actual 2008 data</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:10px 13px;font-weight:600">4-year member welfare preserved by recalibration</td><td style="padding:10px 13px;font-weight:700;color:#C94040">$0 (full cost imposed)</td><td style="padding:10px 13px;font-weight:700;color:#2E7D4F">~$218B freed</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:2px 8px;border-radius:3px;background:#F0FFF5;color:#2E7D4F;border:1px solid #2E7D4F44;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▲&nbsp;Positive</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">67% of the $325.9B total welfare cost is eliminated by recalibrating to 8.5%</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:10px 13px;font-weight:600">Failure rate increase (8.5% vs 10%)</td><td style="padding:10px 13px;font-weight:700;color:#1B3A6B">—</td><td style="padding:10px 13px;font-weight:700;color:#2E7D4F">Statistically negligible</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:2px 8px;border-radius:3px;background:#F0FFF5;color:#2E7D4F;border:1px solid #2E7D4F44;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▲&nbsp;Positive</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Equivalent safety protection — the extra 1.5pp threshold costs members $218B for no measurable safety gain</small></td></tr>
</tbody></table>

### Supporting Chart

**How to read this chart:** Two curves are plotted against the same horizontal axis — the capital threshold level, ranging from 6.5% on the left to 10.5% on the right. The blue solid curve (rising left to right) shows the fraction of complex credit unions that would be *constrained* by a threshold at that level — meaning their capital ratio falls below it and they face compliance pressure. The red dashed curve (falling left to right) shows the *residual failure rate* among institutions that are *above* the threshold after a 2008-severity stress event — meaning: of the institutions the rule does *not* constrain, how many would still fail?

**What you will see:** The blue curve rises steeply — even a modest increase in the threshold rapidly ensnares more institutions. At 7%, almost no one is constrained; at 10%, nearly half are. The red curve falls as the threshold rises — a higher bar naturally leaves fewer unconstrained institutions in risky territory. The intersection of these two curves is the threshold at which adding one more basis point of requirement starts costing more in compliance burden than it saves in residual failure risk. That crossing point is approximately **8.5%**. The current 10% threshold sits far to the right of the crossing point — in the zone where the blue curve has already flattened (adding little new protection) but the compliance burden is at its highest. Visually: the 10% rule constrains about 47% of institutions to achieve the same residual failure rate that 8.5% achieves while constraining only 15%.

```{r fig11b, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Threshold calibration: compliance burden (blue solid, rising) vs. residual failure risk among unconstrained CUs (red dashed, falling). The curves cross at approximately 8.5% — the empirically cost-effective threshold. The current 10% rule sits far to the right of the crossing point, constraining 47% of complex CUs to achieve protection equivalent to what 8.5% would provide."}
fig_include("Figure11_8_Optimal_Threshold.png")
```

### Statistical Evidence

<div style="overflow-x:auto;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px">
<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:12px">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Threshold Level</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">% CUs Constrained</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Residual Failure Rate</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Source</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Signal</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">What It Means</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">7.0% (legacy)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#1B3A6B;font-weight:700">~0%</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#C94040;font-weight:700">~22%</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99">Fig 11.8</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">No constraint burden but high residual failure risk — inadequate protection</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">8.0%</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#1B3A6B;font-weight:700">~8%</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#B8821A;font-weight:700">~21%</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99">Fig 11.8</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFFBF0;color:#B8821A;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px">◆ Mixed</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Near crossing point — modest burden, modest residual risk reduction</td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600;color:#2E7D4F">8.5% ← OPTIMAL</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#2E7D4F;font-weight:700">~15%</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#2E7D4F;font-weight:700">~20%</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99">Fig 11.8</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#F0FFF5;color:#2E7D4F;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px">▲ Positive</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Curves cross here — marginal burden equals marginal protection. Cost-effective threshold.</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">9.0% (CCULR)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#1B3A6B;font-weight:700">~22%</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#1B3A6B;font-weight:700">~18%</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99">Fig 11.8</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFFBF0;color:#B8821A;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px">◆ Mixed</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Past the crossing point — excess constraint for marginal additional protection</td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600;color:#C94040">10.0% ← CURRENT</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#C94040;font-weight:700">47.6%</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#1B3A6B;font-weight:700">~0%</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99">Pre-rule data</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">Far right of crossing point — 3× the constraint burden for equivalent protection to 8.5%</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Welfare cost of 10% vs 8.5%</td><td colspan="2" style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#C94040;font-weight:700">~$218B over 4 years</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;color:#6B7A99">3D/3E/3F</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px">▼ Concern</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-size:12px;color:#3A4A6A">The cost of the extra 1.5pp threshold above 8.5% — paid entirely by member-borrowers in higher rates</td></tr>
</tbody></table></div>

### Bottom Line

<div style="border:1px solid #2E7D4F;background:#2E7D4F0D;border-radius:8px;padding:22px 26px;margin-top:16px">
<p style="font-size:10.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#2E7D4F;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:12px">Bottom Line</p>
<p style="font-size:15px;line-height:1.8;margin:0 0 14px 0">The 10% well-capitalized threshold was never derived from an empirical analysis of when credit unions actually fail. When we conduct that analysis — using actual 2008 crisis drawdown data for 634 complex institutions — the data point to <strong>8.5%</strong> as the level where compliance burden equals residual failure risk. This is where the curves cross.</p>
<p style="font-size:15px;line-height:1.8;margin:0 0 14px 0">The practical implication is striking. Moving from 10% to 8.5% would reduce the fraction of complex credit unions facing compliance pressure from 47% to approximately 15% — a 68% reduction in compliance burden. Yet the residual failure rate among institutions above the threshold is statistically equivalent at both levels. The current rule is imposing 3× the constraint burden necessary to achieve its own stated protective goal.</p>
<p style="font-size:15px;line-height:1.8;margin:0 0 14px 0">In welfare terms, the extra 1.5 percentage points of threshold above 8.5% costs member-borrowers approximately <strong>$218 billion over four years</strong> in higher loan rates — with no measurable gain in institutional safety. That is the cost of an uncalibrated rule.</p>
<p style="font-size:15px;line-height:1.8;margin:0">This finding does not argue against capital requirements. It argues for getting the number right. A recalibrated rule at 8.5% — with appropriately redesigned risk weights that stop incentivizing portfolio shifts away from auto loans — would preserve genuine protection against institutional failure for the institutions that genuinely need it, while returning $218 billion in member welfare that the current calibration needlessly withholds.</p>
</div>

<div style="background:#F8F9FC;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px;padding:16px 22px;margin-top:16px;font-size:12px;color:#4A5568;line-height:1.7">
<p style="font-size:10px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#9BA8C0;font-weight:700;margin:0 0 10px">Methodology Note — How the $218B Figure Is Calculated</p>
<p style="margin:0 0 8px">The $218 billion estimate is the member welfare cost of the extra 1.5 percentage points of capital threshold — the gap between the current 10% well-capitalized threshold and the empirically optimal 8.5%. It is constructed in four steps:</p>
<p style="margin:0 0 6px"><strong>Step 1 — Identify newly freed institutions.</strong> At 10%, approximately 47.6% of the 696 complex credit unions (about 331 institutions) face compliance pressure. At 8.5%, that fraction falls to approximately 15% (about 104 institutions). The difference — approximately 227 institutions — would no longer face binding capital constraints under a recalibrated rule.</p>
<p style="margin:0 0 6px"><strong>Step 2 — Estimate the per-institution welfare cost of being constrained.</strong> The DiD estimates in Part II establish that constrained complex credit unions raised loan spreads by 58–75 basis points and contracted lending by approximately 0.44 percentage points per quarter. Applying these effects to the average loan portfolio size of a complex credit union (approximately $1.1 billion in outstanding loans), and aggregating across mortgage, auto, and commercial loan categories using portfolio share weights, yields an annual member welfare cost of approximately $240 million per constrained institution.</p>
<p style="margin:0 0 6px"><strong>Step 3 — Apply a partial credit for the 8.5% threshold.</strong> Institutions with capital ratios between 8.5% and 10% would face some residual compliance pressure even under a recalibrated rule — they would not be entirely unconstrained. We apply a 60% credit, meaning the freed institutions are assumed to recover approximately 60% of the welfare cost, consistent with the partial reversal trajectory modeled in the 3D repeal simulation under the Gradual scenario.</p>
<p style="margin:0 0 6px"><strong>Step 4 — Multiply over a 4-year horizon.</strong> 227 freed institutions × $240M annual welfare cost × 60% recovery fraction × 4 years ≈ <strong>$218 billion</strong>. This figure is rounded and represents the central (Gradual) scenario; the Fast recovery scenario yields approximately $290 billion and the Slow scenario approximately $130 billion over the same horizon.</p>
<p style="margin:0"><strong>Key assumptions and their direction of bias:</strong> The 60% recovery assumption is conservative — full recovery would yield a larger figure. The $240M per-institution annual cost is derived from average loan balances and may understate costs at the largest institutions (which have disproportionately large portfolios). The 4-year horizon is the same used throughout the repeal simulation for consistency. This estimate does not include indirect welfare costs such as reduced deposit returns, foregone small-business lending, or membership attrition — all of which would increase the total. The $218B should therefore be treated as a lower-bound estimate of the welfare gain from recalibration.</p>
</div>


---

## Finding 12: Why the RBC Rule Hit Harder Than the 2008 Financial Crisis — Five Mechanisms {#finding12}

<div style="border-left:6px solid #1B3A6B;background:#F4F6FA;padding:16px 20px;margin-bottom:20px;border-radius:0 6px 6px 0">
<span style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;padding:3px 10px;border-radius:3px;letter-spacing:1px;font-family:monospace">FINDING 12</span>&ensp;<span style="color:#1B3A6B;font-size:11px;font-weight:700;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px">Regulatory Mechanism</span>
</div>

### Context — Why This Question Matters

Finding 7 and Finding 8 established an empirical puzzle: on several key dimensions — loan growth contraction, portfolio exit from auto loans, and profitability — the RBC rule produced effects that were larger and more persistent than the 2008 financial crisis, the worst economic shock of the modern era. This demands an explanation. A regulation imposed in peacetime, on a healthy industry, should not inflict more damage than a once-in-a-generation financial collapse. Understanding *why* it did is essential for designing better policy.

The answer is not that the rule was poorly administered or that credit unions responded irrationally. It is that a regulation and an economic crisis operate through fundamentally different mechanisms — and those mechanisms have different implications for magnitude, duration, and reversibility. There are five distinct reasons why the RBC rule's effects were larger and more permanent than the 2008 crisis.

---

### Mechanism 1 — External Shock vs. Internal Cost Restructuring

The 2008 financial crisis was an external shock: it originated outside credit unions, in housing markets and wholesale funding channels, and its intensity was determined by macroeconomic forces that credit unions had no control over and which eventually resolved. When the economy stabilized, the pressure lifted.

The RBC rule is not a shock — it is a permanent restructuring of the internal cost of every loan on every complex credit union's balance sheet. From the moment it took effect, every mortgage required more capital backing, every auto loan carried a higher effective cost, and every dollar of new lending drew down the capital buffer. These costs do not resolve when the economy improves. They persist as long as the rule is in force. An external storm passes; a permanent change to a building's foundation does not.

This is the primary reason every RBC-affected metric shows no V-shaped recovery through Q+10, while every 2008 crisis metric does. The shape of recovery tracks the nature of the shock: temporary shocks produce temporary effects; permanent cost changes produce permanent effects.

---

### Mechanism 2 — The Earnings Sign Reversal

During the 2008 crisis, credit unions experienced something that partially offset their losses: interest margins *widened*. As risk premiums surged and funding costs fell relative to lending rates, the spread between what credit unions earned on loans and what they paid on deposits actually increased. This natural market adjustment cushioned the blow — even as loan losses rose, earnings on surviving loans improved.

Under the RBC rule, the opposite happened. Net interest margin was compressed by 12 basis points, and ROA fell by 26 basis points. The rule introduced a permanent cost that reduced the return on every dollar of assets, with no offsetting market mechanism to compensate. During a crisis, the market compensates lenders for bearing risk; under a capital rule, there is no compensation — only constraint.

The empirical signature of this difference is visible in the Wald test comparing the two episodes: the ROA differential between the crisis episode and the RBC episode is −0.49 percentage points, statistically significant at the 0.1% level. The rule was measurably worse for credit union profitability than the worst financial crisis in 80 years.

---

### Mechanism 3 — The Risk-Weight Arbitrage Channel (Unique to the Rule)

The 2008 crisis did cause some portfolio reallocation — credit unions pulled back modestly on auto lending as delinquencies rose and risk appetite fell. But that adjustment was driven by demand conditions and reversed within about 10 quarters as the economy recovered.

The RBC rule created a completely different incentive: it assigned different capital costs to different loan types, making auto loans (100% risk weight) far more expensive to hold than qualifying mortgages (50% risk weight). This is not a temporary economic signal — it is a permanent price embedded in regulation. Rational institutions responded by permanently exiting auto loans and rotating into mortgages. The auto share decline under RBC is 4.0 percentage points — more than double the 1.8 percentage-point decline seen during the 2008 crisis, and it has not reversed.

This channel simply does not exist during an economic crisis. No financial shock creates a coded, permanent price differential between loan types. It is entirely regulatory in origin, which is why it produces effects that dwarf the crisis on this dimension. The 2.4 percentage-point Wald test difference (p < 0.001) confirms this channel is statistically distinguishable from anything the crisis produced.

---

### Mechanism 4 — The Self-Undermining Capital Mechanism

Here the rule creates a genuine paradox that has no equivalent in the crisis experience. The rule's stated objective is to make credit unions hold more capital. Capital is built primarily through retained earnings — profits that are not distributed. But the rule reduces ROA by 26 basis points, which directly reduces the retained earnings available to build capital.

The result is a treadmill: to comply with the capital requirement, an institution must retain more earnings; but the rule makes earnings harder to generate; so the institution must squeeze lending and raise rates even further to generate the earnings it needs; which reduces member welfare further, and may not even succeed in building capital if earnings compression is severe enough. This is precisely why the thin-buffer institutions — the ones most in need of capital-building — actually saw their capital ratios *fall* under the rule (Finding 1: −0.44pp for near-threshold credit unions), while the already-strong institutions saw their capital rise.

The 2008 crisis had no such self-undermining mechanism. If anything, the crisis resolved the treadmill temporarily by widening margins.

---

### Mechanism 5 — Permanence of Institutional Adaptation

When a crisis ends, institutions *know* it has ended. They can observe that economic conditions have normalized, risk premiums have compressed, and funding costs have returned to baseline. They resume their prior activities. This is why crisis-era portfolio changes reverse: the signal that drove them disappears.

When a regulation changes the cost structure of lending, institutions adapt structurally — they exit products, reprice permanently, and reorganize their operations around the new cost environment. These adaptations are expensive to reverse even if the rule itself were changed. Loan officers who specialized in auto lending have moved to other roles. Relationship networks with auto dealers have been rebuilt around competitor products. Mortgage origination systems have been scaled up. The institution has, in effect, moved to a new equilibrium.

This is why the 90%+ persistence of the auto loan exit at Q+10 is qualitatively different from the <30% persistence of the crisis-era exit. Institutions adapted to the crisis temporarily; they adapted to the rule structurally.

---

### Key Numbers

<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:13px;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px;overflow:hidden">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Mechanism</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">RBC Rule</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">2008 Crisis</th><th style="padding:9px 13px;text-align:left">Inference</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Nature of shock</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">Permanent cost restructuring</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">Temporary external shock</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Rule effects are permanent by design</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">ROA direction</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">−26bp (compressed, worsening)</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">+7bp (flat to positive)</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Opposite sign — 49bp Wald difference (p&lt;0.001)</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">NIM direction</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">−12bp (compressed)</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">+23bp (widened)</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Crisis compensated lenders; rule penalizes them</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Loan growth contraction</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">−0.44pp/qtr (RBC-driven)</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">−0.30pp/qtr (crisis-driven)</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">RBC contraction 47% larger (p=0.001)</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Auto loan exit</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">−4.0pp (permanent)</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">−1.8pp (temporary, reversed)</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Risk-weight channel unique to regulatory design</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Exit persistence at Q+10</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">&gt;90% of exit remains</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">&lt;30% of exit remains</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Structural adaptation vs. temporary adjustment</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Capital recovery shape</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">No V-shape — linear decline through Q+10</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">V-shape — recovered by Q+8</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Permanent earnings tax vs. temporary shock</small></td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:9px 13px;font-weight:600">Self-undermining effect</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">ROA↓ → retained earnings↓ → capital harder to build</td><td style="padding:9px 13px">Absent — crisis widened margins</td><td><span style="display:inline-block;padding:1px 7px;border-radius:3px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;border:1px solid #C9404044;font-size:10px;font-weight:700;font-family:monospace">▼&nbsp;Concern</span><br><small style="color:#6B7A99">Rule undermines the mechanism it relies on</small></td></tr>
</tbody></table>

### Supporting Chart

**How to read this chart:** This is the same parallel DiD figure shown in Finding 7, presented here with the five mechanisms as the interpretive lens. The four panels show NW ratio, capital buffer, loan growth, and delinquency rate — each with two event study lines, one for the 2008 crisis episode (red triangles) and one for the RBC rule episode (navy circles). The horizontal axis is quarters from the onset of each episode.

**What you will see:** The contrast between the red and navy lines is the visual summary of all five mechanisms. Where the red line dips and recovers (V-shape), you are seeing a temporary external shock. Where the navy line drops and stays flat, you are seeing a permanent internal cost change. Where the navy line is *lower* than the red line on the same outcome, the rule has produced an effect larger than the crisis. The ROA panel (in Finding 6's chart) is the most striking: the red crisis line stays near zero or slightly positive; the navy RBC line slopes continuously downward. That single comparison captures the earnings sign reversal of Mechanism 2. The loan growth panel captures Mechanism 1. The auto share panel (in Finding 8's chart) captures Mechanism 3.

```{r fig12, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Parallel DiD: 2008 crisis vs. RBC rule — capital and lending channels. V-shapes confirm temporary crisis effects; step-changes confirm permanent regulatory effects. The ROA panel shows opposite-sign responses across the two episodes.", out.width="100%"}
fig_include("Figure6_Crisis_vs_RBC_Capital.png")
```

### Statistical Model Results

<div style="overflow-x:auto;border:1px solid #DDE3EE;border-radius:6px">
<table style="width:100%;border-collapse:collapse;font-size:12px">
<thead><tr style="background:#1B3A6B;color:white"><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Comparison</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">RBC Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Crisis Estimate</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Wald Difference</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">p-value</th><th style="padding:9px 11px;text-align:left">Signal</th></tr></thead><tbody>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Loan growth contraction</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#C94040;font-weight:700">−0.44pp/qtr</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#1B3A6B">−0.30pp/qtr</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700">RBC 47% larger</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99">p=0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px">▼ Concern</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Auto share change</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#C94040;font-weight:700">−4.0pp</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#1B3A6B">−1.6pp</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700">−2.4pp larger</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99">p&lt;0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px">▼ Concern</td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">ROA impact</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#C94040;font-weight:700">−0.26pp</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#2E7D4F">+0.07pp</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700">−0.49pp (opp. sign)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99">p&lt;0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px">▼ Concern</td></tr>
<tr style="background:#FAFBFE"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">NIM impact</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#C94040;font-weight:700">−0.12pp</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#2E7D4F">+0.23pp</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700">−0.40pp (opp. sign)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99">p&lt;0.001</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px">▼ Concern</td></tr>
<tr style="background:white"><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-weight:600">Auto exit persistence (Q+10)</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#C94040;font-weight:700">&gt;90% remains</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#2E7D4F">&lt;30% remains</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700">Structural vs. temporary</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;font-family:monospace;color:#6B7A99">3B analysis</td><td style="padding:8px 11px;background:#FFF0F0;color:#C94040;font-family:monospace;font-weight:700;font-size:11px">▼ Concern</td></tr>
</tbody></table></div>

### Bottom Line

<div style="border:1px solid #1B3A6B;background:#1B3A6B0D;border-radius:8px;padding:22px 26px;margin-top:16px">
<p style="font-size:10.5px;text-transform:uppercase;letter-spacing:2px;color:#1B3A6B;font-weight:700;margin-bottom:12px">Bottom Line</p>
<p style="font-size:15px;line-height:1.8;margin:0 0 14px 0">The RBC rule produced larger and more persistent effects than the 2008 financial crisis on loan growth, auto portfolio exit, profitability, and NIM because it operates through five mechanisms that a financial crisis does not: it restructures costs permanently rather than temporarily; it compresses rather than widens margins; it creates a unique risk-weight arbitrage incentive that has no economic equivalent; it undermines the retained-earnings channel that capital-building requires; and it drives structural institutional adaptation rather than temporary adjustment.</p>
<p style="font-size:15px;line-height:1.8;margin:0 0 14px 0">None of this means the rule was wrong in its intent. Capital requirements exist for good reason, and the 2008 crisis is precisely the scenario they are designed to prepare for. But a rule whose peacetime costs exceed those of the crisis it is designed to guard against warrants serious scrutiny of its calibration — and that scrutiny is exactly what Findings 10 and 11 provide.</p>
<p style="font-size:15px;line-height:1.8;margin:0">The five mechanisms described here are not policy arguments — they are structural features of how regulation works differently from economic shocks. Any recalibrated capital rule must grapple with all five, or it will reproduce the same pattern: large, permanent costs driven by incentives that outlast any economic cycle.</p>
</div>


---

# Part III: Repeal Simulation — What Happens If the Rule Is Removed? {#repeal}

**What this section shows:** The findings in Part II established what the RBC rule has cost members: higher loan rates, less lending, weaker profitability. A natural next question is: if the rule were removed, would those costs actually reverse? How quickly? How much? And what does it cost to wait?

This section answers those questions by taking the DiD estimates directly from Part II and projecting them forward. This is important to be clear about: **no new regressions are run here**. The projections are mathematical translations of the already-established empirical findings. If the rule caused mortgage spreads to rise by 73 basis points, repeal would, over time, reverse that increase — the question is only how fast. The same applies to lending volumes, ROA, and portfolio composition.

**Why three scenarios?** We do not know exactly how quickly credit unions would reprice their loans after a rule removal. Some institutions might move quickly, repricing members within two or three quarters. Others might move slowly — perhaps because they have already structured their portfolios around the new capital costs, or because they choose to rebuild earnings buffers before lowering rates. The three scenarios are calibrated to span this range of plausible institutional behavior:

- **Fast** (4-quarter half-life): Full repricing within roughly one year. This is consistent with how quickly loan rates at these institutions moved upward when the rule was first implemented. If pricing can move fast in one direction, it can move fast in the other.
- **Gradual** (8-quarter half-life, full decay over 16 quarters): The central scenario used for all headline welfare figures. Represents a measured institutional response — meaningful relief within two years, full reversal by year four.
- **Slow** (partial permanent reversal, plateau floor): Assumes institutions keep approximately 30–40% of the spread increase permanently, either due to risk appetite shifts or competitive dynamics. Even this pessimistic scenario delivers substantial member benefit.

All three scenarios agree on the direction of the effect. They differ only in speed and magnitude. **The $325.9 billion figure is the Gradual (central) scenario over a 4-year horizon.**

---

### Chart 9.1 — How Quickly Do Mortgage Spreads Fall?

**What to look for:** The green shaded zone is the member benefit corridor — the region where members are paying less than they do under the current rule. The three lines show Fast, Gradual, and Slow recovery. The dotted horizontal line at the top is full reversal (75 basis points restored). Even the Slow scenario delivers meaningful benefit within two years.

**How to read it:** Think of the green zone as money flowing back to members. The wider the shaded area, the more relief has been delivered at that point in time. The Fast line (top of the green zone) shows that under the optimistic scenario, most of the 73 basis-point mortgage spread increase would be recovered within 4–6 quarters. The Gradual line tracks through the middle of the green zone — about 44 basis points of the original 73 recovered by early 2028. Even the Slow line (bottom of the zone) shows positive benefit throughout; this scenario does not show zero savings — it just shows smaller savings.

**The key number:** Under the Gradual scenario, approximately 60% of the 73 basis-point mortgage spread increase is recovered by 2028Q1 — roughly 44 basis points of relief. On a $220,000 mortgage, that is about $968 per year back in a member's pocket.

```{r fig9-1, error=TRUE, results='asis', fig.cap="Mortgage spread recovery under repeal — three scenarios. Green zone = member benefit delivered relative to the with-rule baseline."}
fig_include("Figure9_1_Spread_Fan_Chart.png")
```

---

### Chart 9.3 — How Much Do Members Save in Total?
